questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Star’s Fingerprint
At the Hillcrest Observatory, students expected to look through the telescope and see planets. Instead, Dr. Naomi Chen handed them printed sheets covered with dark lines. The lines came from starlight that had passed through a spectroscope, an instrument that separates light into different colors. To the students, the sheets looked like bar codes. To Chen, they were evidence.
Every chemical element absorbs or emits light in a particular pattern. When scientists study the pattern from a star, they can identify some of the elements in its outer layers. Hydrogen, helium, calcium, and sodium each leave different marks. This is why astronomers sometimes call a spectrum a star’s fingerprint.
Chen explained that a spectrum can reveal more than composition. The strength of certain lines can suggest temperature. If the whole pattern shifts slightly toward red or blue, scientists can tell whether the star or galaxy is moving away from or toward Earth. These conclusions do not come from guessing. They come from comparing observed patterns with patterns measured in laboratories.
One student asked why anyone should care about lines on paper when the sky itself was so beautiful. Chen smiled and pointed to the telescope dome. “Looking is the beginning,” she said. “Measuring lets us ask what the light is telling us.”
By the end of the evening, the students still admired the night sky. But they also understood that distant stars could be studied through evidence carried by their light.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Spectra allow scientists to study stars by interpreting patterns in their light.
choices.B: Telescopes are useful only when students look at planets directly.
choices.C: A star’s spectrum shows only how beautiful the night sky looks.
choices.D: Astronomers identify elements by guessing from a star’s color alone.

questions.correctAnswerText: Spectra allow scientists to study stars by interpreting patterns in their light.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how patterns in starlight reveal elements, temperature clues, and motion.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Star’s Fingerprint.”
Why is a spectrum compared to a fingerprint?

choices.A: It contains patterns that can help identify elements in a star.
choices.B: It records the handprints of students at the observatory.
choices.C: It shows the exact size of every planet.
choices.D: It proves that all stars contain the same elements.

questions.correctAnswerText: It contains patterns that can help identify elements in a star.
questions.explanation: Like a fingerprint, a spectrum has identifying patterns that can reveal composition.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Star’s Fingerprint.”
Which TWO things can scientists learn from a star’s spectrum?

choices.A: Some elements in the star’s outer layers
choices.B: Whether the star or galaxy is moving toward or away from Earth
choices.C: The names of students visiting the observatory
choices.D: The number of telescopes in the building
choices.E: The exact shape of every planet orbiting the star

questions.correctAnswerText: Some elements in the star’s outer layers | Whether the star or galaxy is moving toward or away from Earth
questions.explanation: The passage says spectra can show elements and motion through red or blue shifts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Star’s Fingerprint.”
What can be inferred from Dr. Chen’s statement, “Looking is the beginning”?

choices.A: Observing the sky is useful, but measurement allows deeper scientific understanding.
choices.B: Students should stop looking at the sky after one night.
choices.C: Beauty is the only thing scientists can learn from stars.
choices.D: Laboratory measurements are unrelated to astronomy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Observing the sky is useful, but measurement allows deeper scientific understanding.
questions.explanation: Dr. Chen’s comment contrasts simple looking with evidence-based measurement.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Star’s Fingerprint.”
In the sentence “To Chen, they were evidence,” evidence most nearly means [D1].

D1: a personal opinion with no support

questions.correctAnswerText: D1=information that supports a conclusion
questions.explanation: In context, evidence means information used to support scientific conclusions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Star’s Fingerprint.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Each chemical element absorbs or emits light in a particular pattern.
tiles.B: A red or blue shift can show motion toward or away from Earth.
tiles.C: Scientists compare star patterns with patterns measured in laboratories.
tiles.D: Students use the spectrum to choose telescope paint colors.

targets.T1: Evidence that spectra can identify elements
targets.T2: Evidence that spectra can reveal movement
targets.T3: Evidence that conclusions are based on comparison

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Each chemical element absorbs or emits light in a particular pattern.; T2=A red or blue shift can show motion toward or away from Earth.; T3=Scientists compare star patterns with patterns measured in laboratories.
questions.explanation: The tiles show how spectra identify elements, reveal motion, and support evidence-based conclusions.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Vendor Invoice Review Procedure
To: Department Coordinators, Purchasing Staff, and Accounts Payable
From: Finance Operations Manager
Subject: Vendor Invoice Review Procedure
Beginning February 18, all vendor invoices must be reviewed before payment is approved. This procedure applies to supplies, repairs, subscriptions, consulting services, and equipment rentals.
The department coordinator must compare each invoice with the original purchase approval or service agreement. Check the vendor name, invoice number, service dates, item description, quantity, rate, tax, and total amount. If the invoice includes charges that were not approved, do not send it to accounts payable until the department manager has reviewed the difference.
Any dispute must be recorded on the Invoice Review Note. Include the invoice number, vendor contact, disputed amount, reason for the dispute, and the person responsible for follow-up. Do not write unclear notes such as “seems wrong.” Instead, describe the issue specifically, such as “invoice lists 12 months of service, but agreement approved 6 months.”
Accounts payable will process complete and approved invoices on Tuesday and Friday. Incomplete invoices will be returned to the department coordinator. Careful review helps prevent overpayment, duplicate payment, and delays when vendors ask about invoice status.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should review vendor invoices before payment
choices.B: To allow staff to approve invoices without checking service agreements
choices.C: To require vendors to rewrite every invoice after payment
choices.D: To tell accounts payable to process incomplete invoices first

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should review vendor invoices before payment
questions.explanation: The document explains how invoices should be compared with approvals or agreements before payment.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Vendor Invoice Review Procedure.”
What should a department coordinator compare an invoice with?

choices.A: The original purchase approval or service agreement
choices.B: The office lunch order
choices.C: The employee vacation calendar
choices.D: The vendor’s advertising slogan

questions.correctAnswerText: The original purchase approval or service agreement
questions.explanation: Coordinators must compare invoices with the purchase approval or service agreement.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Vendor Invoice Review Procedure.”
Which TWO details should be included on the Invoice Review Note?

choices.A: The disputed amount
choices.B: The reason for the dispute
choices.C: The coordinator’s favorite vendor
choices.D: The office temperature
choices.E: The color of the invoice paper

questions.correctAnswerText: The disputed amount | The reason for the dispute
questions.explanation: The Invoice Review Note must include the disputed amount and the reason for the dispute.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Vendor Invoice Review Procedure.”
Why is the note “seems wrong” not acceptable?

choices.A: It does not clearly describe the specific issue with the invoice.
choices.B: It includes too many details about the invoice.
choices.C: It proves that the vendor has already corrected the problem.
choices.D: It replaces the need to include the invoice number.

questions.correctAnswerText: It does not clearly describe the specific issue with the invoice.
questions.explanation: The procedure requires specific notes rather than vague comments.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Vendor Invoice Review Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning February 18, all vendor invoices must be reviewed before payment is approved.”
choices.B: “This procedure applies to supplies, repairs, subscriptions, consulting services, and equipment rentals.”
choices.C: “Careful review helps prevent overpayment, duplicate payment, and delays when vendors ask about invoice status.”
choices.D: “Accounts payable will process complete and approved invoices on Tuesday and Friday.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Careful review helps prevent overpayment, duplicate payment, and delays when vendors ask about invoice status.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains why invoice review matters.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees
Employers should provide a small amount of paid bereavement leave for part-time employees. When a close family member dies, workers may need time to attend a funeral, handle arrangements, travel, or simply manage the first days of grief. Full-time employees often receive paid bereavement leave, but part-time employees may be expected to miss pay or work through the loss.
A basic policy would be fair and practical. It could provide one to three paid shifts after the death of an immediate family member, with clear documentation rules. The policy would not need to cover every situation in the same way. Employers could define eligible relationships and allow managers to review unusual circumstances.
Paid bereavement leave can also support the workplace. An employee who is forced to work immediately after a major loss may be distracted, exhausted, or unable to communicate with customers and coworkers effectively. A short period away from work can help the employee return more prepared.
Some employers may worry about cost or misuse. Those concerns are understandable, but they can be managed with limits, documentation, and supervisor review. Part-time employees are still part of the organization. A humane policy during a rare and serious event shows that the workplace values people, not only schedules.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should offer limited paid bereavement leave to part-time employees because serious loss affects both workers and workplaces.
choices.B: Part-time employees should never be allowed to miss work for family events.
choices.C: Bereavement leave should replace all other employee benefits.
choices.D: Employers should provide unlimited paid leave for every personal inconvenience.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should offer limited paid bereavement leave to part-time employees because serious loss affects both workers and workplaces.
questions.explanation: The author argues that part-time workers need limited leave during serious loss and that workplaces benefit when employees return more prepared.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that bereavement leave can help the workplace?

choices.A: An employee forced to work immediately after a loss may be distracted or exhausted.
choices.B: Employers can define eligible relationships.
choices.C: Documentation rules may be included in the policy.
choices.D: Full-time employees often receive paid bereavement leave.

questions.correctAnswerText: An employee forced to work immediately after a loss may be distracted or exhausted.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that bereavement leave can help the workplace.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees.”
Which TWO limits or safeguards does the author suggest?

choices.A: Clear documentation rules
choices.B: Supervisor review
choices.C: Unlimited leave with no explanation
choices.D: Excluding every part-time worker from the policy
choices.E: Requiring employees to work during funerals

questions.correctAnswerText: Clear documentation rules | Supervisor review
questions.explanation: The author suggests documentation and supervisor review as safeguards.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees.”
How does the author respond to concerns about cost or misuse?

choices.A: By saying those concerns can be managed with limits, documentation, and review
choices.B: By claiming cost never matters for any employer
choices.C: By saying misuse is impossible in every workplace
choices.D: By recommending that employers remove all supervisor oversight

questions.correctAnswerText: By saying those concerns can be managed with limits, documentation, and review
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges cost and misuse concerns, then proposes limits and oversight.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 3: [D1]

D1: A regional employer reported fewer last-minute resignations after extending limited bereavement leave to part-time staff.
The employee handbook has a blue cover and a table of contents.
Some employees prefer to request leave by email.

questions.correct: D1=A regional employer reported fewer last-minute resignations after extending limited bereavement leave to part-time staff.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A regional employer reported fewer last-minute resignations after extending limited bereavement leave to part-time staff.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by connecting the policy to workplace stability.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Provide Paid Bereavement Leave for Part-Time Employees.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: A short period of paid leave can help some grieving part-time employees return to work more prepared.
choices.B: Every part-time employee experiences the same family situation.
choices.C: Paid leave policies should have no limits.
choices.D: Grief has no effect on work performance.

questions.correctAnswerText: A short period of paid leave can help some grieving part-time employees return to work more prepared.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that limited leave can meaningfully support workers after serious loss.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The First Frame
For almost a year, Clara had worked at the framing shop without cutting a mat for a customer order. She cleaned glass, wrapped finished frames in brown paper, and sorted corner samples by color. The cutting table belonged to Ben, whose hands seemed to know exactly how much pressure a blade needed.
On Friday, Ben placed a watercolor on the table. It showed a small yellow house under a stormy sky. “Simple white mat,” he said. “Customer wants it ready tomorrow.”
Clara measured the watercolor twice. The border needed to be even, but not so wide that the painting looked lost. She marked the back of the mat board with a pencil and lined up the straightedge. Her first cut was too shallow. The second went cleanly through, but her hand shook at the corner.
Ben did not take the blade from her. “Corners show patience,” he said.
Clara slowed down. She cut the remaining sides, lifted the center piece away, and placed the watercolor behind the opening. The white border made the yellow house brighter, as if someone had turned on a lamp inside it.
The next afternoon, the customer picked up the frame and held it at arm’s length. “It looks calmer now,” she said.
Clara understood what she meant. The storm was still in the painting, but the frame had given it a place to rest.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Clara must complete a precise framing task for a real customer order.
choices.B: Clara tries to convince Ben to close the framing shop.
choices.C: Clara refuses to measure the watercolor before cutting.
choices.D: The customer asks Clara to repaint the yellow house.

questions.correctAnswerText: Clara must complete a precise framing task for a real customer order.
questions.explanation: The excerpt centers on Clara’s first customer mat-cutting responsibility.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Frame.”
Why does Clara measure the watercolor twice?

choices.A: She wants the mat border to fit the painting correctly.
choices.B: She wants to prove the customer chose the wrong picture.
choices.C: She is trying to avoid wrapping finished frames.
choices.D: She believes measurements are not useful in framing.

questions.correctAnswerText: She wants the mat border to fit the painting correctly.
questions.explanation: Clara measures carefully because the border must be even and appropriate for the watercolor.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Frame.”
What does Ben mean when he says, “Corners show patience”?

choices.A: Careful, slow work is needed where mistakes are most visible.
choices.B: Corners are the only part of the mat that customers cannot see.
choices.C: Clara should cut faster so the corner looks natural.
choices.D: Paintings with stormy skies do not need frames.

questions.correctAnswerText: Careful, slow work is needed where mistakes are most visible.
questions.explanation: Ben’s advice means corners require patience because errors there are noticeable.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The First Frame.”
Which TWO details show the effect of Clara’s finished work?

choices.A: The white border makes the yellow house look brighter.
choices.B: The customer says the picture looks calmer.
choices.C: Clara sorts corner samples by color.
choices.D: Ben places the watercolor on the table.
choices.E: Clara’s first cut is too shallow.

questions.correctAnswerText: The white border makes the yellow house look brighter. | The customer says the picture looks calmer.
questions.explanation: These details show the visual and emotional effect of Clara’s finished work.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Frame.”
What does the final sentence suggest?

choices.A: Clara sees how framing can change the way a picture is experienced.
choices.B: Clara believes the storm has disappeared from the painting.
choices.C: Clara decides that customer comments are never useful.
choices.D: Clara realizes that the frame has damaged the watercolor.

questions.correctAnswerText: Clara sees how framing can change the way a picture is experienced.
questions.explanation: The final sentence shows Clara understanding the frame’s effect on the painting.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Frame.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Clara’s hand shakes at the corner.
tiles.B: Ben tells Clara that corners show patience.
tiles.C: The customer says the picture looks calmer.
tiles.D: Clara throws away the watercolor before framing it.

targets.T3: Evidence that the finished frame succeeds

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Clara’s hand shakes at the corner.; T2=Ben tells Clara that corners show patience.; T3=The customer says the picture looks calmer.
questions.explanation: The tiles show uncertainty, guidance, and a successful result.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Credit Report Dispute Notice
A credit report dispute notice explains the result of a consumer’s request to investigate information on a credit report. A consumer may file a dispute when a report lists an account, balance, payment status, address, or personal detail that seems inaccurate. The notice tells the consumer what was investigated and what changed, if anything.
One important detail is the outcome. The reporting agency may delete the item, update it, verify it as accurate, or state that more information is needed. If an item is verified, that does not always mean the consumer agrees with it. It means the company that supplied the information confirmed it according to the investigation process.
Consumers should read the explanation and compare it with their own records. A bank statement, payoff letter, police report, identity theft report, or letter from a creditor may help support a future dispute. If the consumer still disagrees after the investigation, the notice may explain how to add a statement to the report or submit additional evidence.
A dispute notice is not the same as a complete credit history lesson. It is a response to a specific question about specific information. Reading it carefully can help consumers decide whether the issue is resolved or whether another step is needed.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A credit report dispute notice explains investigation results and possible next steps.
choices.B: A credit report dispute notice always removes every negative item.
choices.C: Consumers should ignore their own records after filing a dispute.
choices.D: A verified item means the consumer must agree with the information.

questions.correctAnswerText: A credit report dispute notice explains investigation results and possible next steps.
questions.explanation: The passage explains outcomes, records, and additional steps after a dispute investigation.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Credit Report Dispute Notice.”
What may happen as a result of a dispute investigation?

choices.A: An item may be deleted, updated, verified, or require more information.
choices.B: The consumer’s credit report may be erased completely.
choices.C: The reporting agency may cancel all bank accounts.
choices.D: Every disputed item must become a new loan.

questions.correctAnswerText: An item may be deleted, updated, verified, or require more information.
questions.explanation: The passage lists these possible investigation outcomes.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Credit Report Dispute Notice.”
Why should consumers compare the notice with their own records?

choices.A: Their records may help them decide whether to submit more evidence or take another step.
choices.B: Their records automatically replace the credit report.
choices.C: Their records are never relevant after a dispute is filed.
choices.D: Their records prove that every verified item is false.

questions.correctAnswerText: Their records may help them decide whether to submit more evidence or take another step.
questions.explanation: Comparing the notice with records helps consumers decide how to respond.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Credit Report Dispute Notice.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that a verified item may still be disputed further?

choices.A: “A consumer may file a dispute when a report lists an account, balance, payment status, address, or personal detail that seems inaccurate.”
choices.B: “If an item is verified, that does not always mean the consumer agrees with it.”
choices.C: “A dispute notice is not the same as a complete credit history lesson.”
choices.D: “The notice tells the consumer what was investigated and what changed, if anything.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “If an item is verified, that does not always mean the consumer agrees with it.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports the idea that a verified item may still be questioned by the consumer.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Credit Report Dispute Notice.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It defines a dispute notice, explains possible outcomes, and gives advice for reviewing the result.
choices.B: It tells a story about a person opening a checking account.
choices.C: It compares credit card rewards programs.
choices.D: It argues that consumers should never file disputes.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines a dispute notice, explains possible outcomes, and gives advice for reviewing the result.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from definition to outcomes to practical advice.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Course Materials Pickup
(1) Students enrolled in the evening accounting course may pick up course materials next week. (2) The packet includes a workbook, calculator guide, syllabus, and practice ledger pages. (3) Students should show a photo ID at the registration counter before receiving the packet. (4) [D1], packets will not be released to friends or family members. (5) The pickup table [D2] located beside Room 112 from 5:00 p.m. to 8:00 p.m. (6) Students should check the packet contents before leaving the building.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “Therefore” connects the ID requirement to the release rule, and “will be located” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Course Materials Pickup.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Students should check the packet contents before leaving the building.
choices.B: Students should perform a check of the contents of the packet before they leave the building.
choices.C: The contents of the packet should be checked by students before the building is left.
choices.D: Students, before leaving from the building, should check the contents that are inside the packet.

questions.correctAnswerText: Students should check the packet contents before leaving the building.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and keeps the original meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The packets on the pickup table needs labels.
choices.B: The list of enrolled students are printed at the counter.
choices.C: The students who attend evening classes receive course materials.
choices.D: The folder of practice pages have been copied.

questions.correctAnswerText: The students who attend evening classes receive course materials.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “students” correctly takes the plural verb “receive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Avery called Jordan after class, she found the missing workbook.
choices.B: Avery called Jordan after class, and Avery found the missing workbook.
choices.C: She found it when Avery called Jordan after class.
choices.D: After class, she called her and found it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Avery called Jordan after class, and Avery found the missing workbook.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Avery” makes clear who found the workbook.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The first shipment of workbooks arrived late. [D1], the instructor moved the first workbook assignment to the following week.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore
questions.explanation: The late shipment caused the instructor to move the assignment.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The packet was complete: the student signed the pickup sheet.
choices.B: The packet was complete the student signed the pickup sheet.
choices.C: The packet was complete, the student signed the pickup sheet.
choices.D: The packet was complete: and the student, signed the pickup sheet.

questions.correctAnswerText: The packet was complete: the student signed the pickup sheet.
questions.explanation: The colon correctly connects a complete sentence to a closely related result.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the workbook was missing.
choices.B: After the registration counter closed.
choices.C: The instructor placed extra syllabus copies on the table.
choices.D: While checking the calculator guide.

questions.correctAnswerText: The instructor placed extra syllabus copies on the table.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The assistant checked the roster and distributes the packets yesterday.
choices.B: The assistant checks the roster and distributed the packets yesterday.
choices.C: The assistant checked the roster and distributed the packets yesterday.
choices.D: The assistant had checked the roster and distributes the packets yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The assistant checked the roster and distributed the packets yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The packet includes a workbook, calculator guide, and whether practice pages are included.
choices.B: The packet includes a workbook, calculator guide, and practice ledger pages.
choices.C: The packet includes working in a book, calculator guide, and to practice ledger pages.
choices.D: The packet includes a workbook, guiding the calculator, and pages that are for practice.

questions.correctAnswerText: The packet includes a workbook, calculator guide, and practice ledger pages.
questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Switch to Reusable Shipping Totes
A parts supplier should switch from single-use cardboard boxes to reusable shipping totes for regular deliveries to its largest customers. The company sends the same types of small parts to the same customer locations every week. Sturdy totes could be delivered full, returned empty on the next truck, cleaned, and used again. This would reduce cardboard waste and the time employees spend breaking down boxes.
Reusable totes could also protect products better. Cardboard weakens when it becomes wet or crushed, while rigid totes keep small parts organized during transport. The company could label totes by customer and route, making receiving faster for customers who handle frequent deliveries.
The change should begin with a pilot program. The supplier could test totes with two nearby customers, track return rates, cleaning time, damage, and customer satisfaction, then decide whether to expand. A limited trial would show whether the benefits are real before the company invests in a large number of totes.
Passage B: Keep Using Cardboard Boxes
The parts supplier should keep using cardboard boxes for regular deliveries. Cardboard is not perfect, but it is flexible. Boxes come in many sizes, can be recycled by customers, and do not need to be returned. Reusable totes create a new system that must be managed carefully.
The return process could become a problem. If customers forget to return totes, the supplier may run short or need to buy replacements. Empty totes take space on trucks and in customer receiving areas. Totes also need cleaning and inspection, especially if they carry parts that leave dust or oil residue. These tasks add labor that may reduce any savings from using less cardboard.
A pilot program might help, but it may not show problems that appear later when more customers are involved. The supplier should first improve cardboard use by choosing better box sizes, reducing extra packing material, and working with customers on recycling. That approach avoids creating a return system that could slow deliveries.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports testing reusable totes, while Passage B argues cardboard remains more flexible and easier to manage.
choices.B: Passage A argues that deliveries should stop, while Passage B argues that customers should make their own parts.
choices.C: Passage A supports removing all labels, while Passage B supports banning recycling.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on astronomy, while Passage B focuses on course materials.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports testing reusable totes, while Passage B argues cardboard remains more flexible and easier to manage.
questions.explanation: Passage A argues for a limited tote trial, while Passage B emphasizes the management advantages of cardboard.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about shipping containers.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: The company sends similar small parts to the same customer locations every week.
choices.B: Empty totes take space on trucks and in receiving areas.
choices.C: Cardboard boxes come in many sizes.
choices.D: Customers may forget to return totes.

questions.correctAnswerText: The company sends similar small parts to the same customer locations every week.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s claim that reusable totes may work for repeated delivery routes.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about shipping containers.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Reusable totes may require extra cleaning and inspection.
choices.B: The return process could create shortages if customers do not send totes back.
choices.C: Reusable totes always damage every part.
choices.D: Cardboard can never be recycled.
choices.E: A return system would eliminate all delivery costs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reusable totes may require extra cleaning and inspection. | The return process could create shortages if customers do not send totes back.
questions.explanation: Passage B uses both points to argue that reusable totes add management problems.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about shipping containers.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses the risk of making a large change too quickly?

choices.A: “The company sends the same types of small parts to the same customer locations every week.”
choices.B: “Reusable totes could also protect products better.”
choices.C: “The change should begin with a pilot program.”
choices.D: “The company could label totes by customer and route.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The change should begin with a pilot program.”
questions.explanation: A pilot program directly addresses the risk of making a large change before testing it.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about shipping containers.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors care about efficient deliveries, but they disagree about whether reusable totes would improve the system.
choices.B: Both authors believe packaging choices do not affect delivery work.
choices.C: Both authors think customers should stop receiving weekly deliveries.
choices.D: Both authors argue that pilots never provide useful information.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors care about efficient deliveries, but they disagree about whether reusable totes would improve the system.
questions.explanation: Both passages focus on delivery efficiency and packaging management, but they support different approaches.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: Data on tote return rates, cleaning labor, product damage, cardboard costs, truck space, and customer satisfaction
choices.B: A list of employee birthdays
choices.C: A history of cardboard colors
choices.D: A survey asking customers which office snacks they prefer

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on tote return rates, cleaning labor, product damage, cardboard costs, truck space, and customer satisfaction
questions.explanation: These data would help evaluate both benefits and concerns.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Placed in sealed envelopes, Morgan counted the course packets before pickup began.

choices.A: Placed in sealed envelopes, Morgan counted the course packets before pickup began.
choices.B: Morgan counted the course packets placed in sealed envelopes before pickup began.
choices.C: Morgan, placed in sealed envelopes, counted the course packets before pickup began.
choices.D: The sealed envelopes counted Morgan’s course packets before pickup began.

questions.correctAnswerText: Morgan counted the course packets placed in sealed envelopes before pickup began.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “course packets,” the items placed in sealed envelopes.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The instructor sent a message. The message listed the pickup hours.

choices.A: The instructor sent a message that listed the pickup hours.
choices.B: The message sent the instructor and listed the pickup hours.
choices.C: Listing the instructor, the pickup hours sent a message.
choices.D: The instructor sent, and the pickup hours listed the message.

questions.correctAnswerText: The instructor sent a message that listed the pickup hours.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The student’s workbook was included in the course packet.
choices.B: The students workbook was included in the course packet.
choices.C: The students’ workbook’s was included in the course packet.
choices.D: The student workbooks’ was included in the course packet.

questions.correctAnswerText: The student’s workbook was included in the course packet.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the workbook belongs to one student.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Renewal Reminders for Annual Subscriptions
Companies that sell annual subscriptions should be required to send customers a clear renewal reminder before charging them for another year. Many subscriptions renew automatically after a customer signs up once. A customer may forget the renewal date, stop using the service, or overlook the cost until the charge appears on a bank statement. A reminder gives the customer a fair chance to decide whether to continue.
The reminder should include the renewal date, amount to be charged, cancellation deadline, and simple instructions for changing or ending the subscription. This information is especially important when the subscription began with a discount and renews at a higher regular price. Customers should not have to search through old emails or account pages to learn when a large charge will occur.
A reminder requirement would not prevent companies from selling subscriptions. It would simply make automatic renewal more transparent. Businesses that provide value should be able to retain customers without relying on forgetfulness. Clear reminders can also reduce refund requests and customer frustration after unexpected charges.
Some companies may worry about extra administrative work. However, most subscription businesses already send marketing emails, payment receipts, and account notices. A clear renewal reminder is a reasonable consumer protection for a charge that may happen only once a year.
Passage B:
Do Not Add Broad Renewal Reminder Rules
Companies should not face a broad legal requirement to send renewal reminders for every annual subscription. Customers already agree to renewal terms when they sign up, and account settings usually show billing dates and cancellation options. People have a responsibility to track services they choose to purchase.
A reminder rule may also create unnecessary costs and confusion. Companies would need to update systems, track message delivery, handle bounced emails, and decide what happens if a reminder is not received. Some customers may assume that a missed reminder cancels the subscription, creating disputes. Small businesses with simple subscription systems may find the requirement harder to manage than large companies do.
There are better ways to address the problem. Companies should display renewal terms clearly at sign-up, provide easy cancellation, and send receipts after charges. Customers can use calendar alerts or banking notifications to track upcoming payments. Regulators can punish hidden terms or difficult cancellation practices without requiring a reminder for every annual subscription.
Automatic renewal is not unfair by itself. It can help customers keep services without interruption. The focus should be on clear terms and easy cancellation, not another required message that customers may ignore.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether companies should be required to send renewal reminders before charging customers for annual subscriptions? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
